citation: Vlahoyiannis A, Aphamis G, Bogdanis GC, Sakkas GK, Andreou E, Giannaki CD. Deconstructing athletes' sleep: A systematic review of the influence of age, sex, athletic expertise, sport type, and season on sleep characteristics. Journal of Sport and Health Science. 2020. doi:10.1016/j.jshs.2020.03.006
abstract: This systematic review aimed to describe objective sleep parameters for athletes under different conditions using only polysomnography, EEG, or actigraphy measures. PubMed and Scopus were searched from inception to April 2019, yielding 81 included studies monitoring 1,830 athletes over 18,958 nights. Athletes slept on average 7.2 ± 1.1 h/night with 86.3% ± 6.8% sleep efficiency (SE), below the AASM consensus for healthy non-athlete adults. Notable sleep deficiencies were found in young/adolescent athletes and during heavy training phases.
tldr: Athletes consistently get less sleep and lower sleep efficiency than recommended, averaging only 7.2 h/night versus the 8+ hours optimal for recovery. Young athletes are the most sleep-deprived subgroup (SE ~80.3%), and sleep duration and quality decline meaningfully during heavy training phases compared to pre-season or competition phases. Sports performance coaches should prioritize sleep monitoring and optimization — especially during high-load training blocks — and should recognize that heavy training periods predictably worsen both sleep duration (by ~36 min vs. pre-season) and efficiency. Position-specific and sport-type differences in sleep architecture exist, highlighting the need for individualized sleep interventions.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA guidelines; PubMed and Scopus searched from inception to April 2, 2019. Included studies measured sleep exclusively via objective tools (polysomnography/EEG and/or actigraphy). 81 studies included, covering 1,830 athletes (230 females, 1,040 males, 560 mixed) across 18,958 recorded nights. Data were extracted for total sleep time (TST), sleep efficiency (SE), sleep onset latency (SOL), wake after sleep onset (WASO), REM, and sleep stages N1–N3. Athletes were categorized by sex, age group (children/adolescents 6–17 yr; young adults 18–24 yr; middle-aged adults 25–40 yr), athletic expertise (elite vs. semi-elite), training season (pre-season, in-season/competition, heavy training), and sport type (aerobic, anaerobic, mixed; individual vs. team). Quality assessed with modified Newcastle-Ottawa Scale.
findings: Athletes slept 7.2 ± 1.1 h/night with 86.3% ± 6.8% SE; all dataset means were <8 h TST. Young athletes had SE of 80.3% ± 8.8% with elevated WASO (74 min). During heavy training phases, sleep duration was 36 min less than pre-season and 42 min less than in-season; SE was 3.0% lower than in-season. Elite and semi-elite athletes had similar TST but differed in SOL (semi-elite: 9.5 vs. elite: 18.4 min) and SE (elite 1.5% lower). Female athletes slept slightly longer (7.5 vs. 7.2 h) with higher SE (89.1% vs. 85.2%). Sleep architecture showed athletes spent 23% in REM, 9.7% N1, 48.7% N2, 18.5% N3. Power/explosiveness sport athletes had longer SOL and less N3 sleep compared to aerobic athletes.
limitations: Only objectively measured data were included, limiting sample sizes for certain subgroups (e.g., female athletes, masters athletes, middle-aged adults). Differences in SE calculation methods across studies could artificially inflate some SE values. No sleep architecture data were available for female athletes at elite or semi-elite levels, or for middle-aged adult athletes. Wearable consumer devices were excluded due to validation concerns. Generalizations about sleep stages should be made with caution given the limited number of PSG/EEG studies.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs should treat sleep as a trainable and monitorable component of recovery. Given that heavy training phases predictably reduce sleep duration by 36–42 min and efficiency by up to 3%, load management and sleep hygiene interventions (e.g., consistent sleep schedules, sleep environment optimization, napping protocols) should be proactively deployed during high-volume training blocks. Actigraphy monitoring is a validated, practical tool for ongoing sleep surveillance in squad settings. Young players are the highest-risk group for insufficient sleep and warrant dedicated sleep education.
athleteDev: Young football athletes (high school and early college) are particularly vulnerable to sleep deficiency, with SE averaging only 80.3% and WASO of ~74 min — well below adult norms. Insufficient sleep impairs motor learning, psychomotor vigilance, hormone regulation, and body composition, all critical for athlete development. Development programs should integrate sleep education, limit early-morning training sessions that cut sleep short, and monitor sleep as a recovery metric alongside HRV and soreness. Sleep extension interventions (targeting >8 h for developing athletes) have demonstrated improvements in reaction time and sport-specific performance in adolescent cohorts.
rtp: Sleep disruption during rehabilitation should be monitored as a recovery metric, as low SE and reduced TST are associated with impaired physiological restoration and hormonal imbalances that suppress muscle growth and tissue repair. The finding that heavy training load reduces sleep quantity and quality suggests that injured players undergoing intensive rehab are at heightened risk for sleep deficiency. Ensuring adequate sleep (>8 h with high SE) during the return-to-play process may accelerate tissue healing and reduce risk of re-injury. Coaches and medical staff should screen for sleep disorders (e.g., sleep apnea) in high-BMI players, as obstructive sleep apnea is prevalent in linemen-type athletes.

citation: May JM, Angileri HS, McLoughlin DE, Owen MM, Terry M, Tjong V. Decreased Concussion Incidence Following the Implementation of the Targeting Rules: An Updated Epidemiology of National Football League Concussions From 2017 to 2022. Cureus. 2023;15(12):e50997. DOI 10.7759/cureus.50997
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study retrospectively reviewed concussions across five NFL seasons (2017-18 through 2021-22) using publicly available injury reports to characterize incidence, games missed, and positional differences. A total of 368 concussions resulting in at least one missed game were identified across 1,275 regular and postseason games. There was a statistically significant 38% decrease in total concussions following the 2017-18 season (p=0.02), coinciding with the introduction of the NFL Targeting rule in 2018, with the 2021-22 season recording the fewest concussions (61) despite the addition of a 17th regular season game.
tldr: NFL concussions dropped 38% after the 2018 Targeting rule was enacted, and games missed per concussion fell 38% as well — suggesting both fewer and less severe head injuries. Defensive backs and tight ends had the highest concussion rates (10.46 and 10.69 per 1,000 AEs respectively), while the defensive line had the highest average games missed per concussion (3.97). The addition of a 17th game in 2021 did not increase total concussions. Offensive and defensive units experienced similar reductions. For football performance staff, these trends validate rule-driven and equipment-driven safety investments, but concussions causing multiple missed games persist, particularly in defensive linemen.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiological study of five NFL seasons (2017-18 to 2021-22). Data extracted from publicly available injury databases (Pro Football Reference, NFL.com, ESPN) and cross-referenced for accuracy. Only concussions resulting in at least one missed regular season or postseason game were included; preseason and training camp concussions were excluded. Concussion incidence normalized per 1,000 athlete exposures (AEs), with 1 AE = 1 game per player. Sub-analyses by year, offensive/defensive unit, position group, BMI, and game week. Single-factor ANOVA used for statistical comparisons; significance set at p ≤ 0.05.
findings: 368 total concussions over five seasons; rate of 0.30 per game and 7.04 per 1,000 AEs. The 2017-18 season had 102 concussions (highest); subsequent four seasons averaged 66.75 (p=0.02, 38% decrease). Total games missed fell 53% post-2017-18 (p=0.05); average games missed per concussion fell from 3.46 to ~2.14. Defensive players had a significantly decreased concussion rate post-2017-18 (p=0.04); offensive players did not reach significance. Defensive backs: 10.46 per 1,000 AEs; tight ends: 10.69 per 1,000 AEs (highest by position). Defensive line: lowest concussion rate (3.70 per 1,000 AEs) but highest average games missed (3.97). Concussion concentration was significantly higher in the final third of the regular season (p=0.03). BMI global maximum at 27, local maximum at 38.
limitations: Only concussions causing at least one missed game were captured, excluding return-same-day or practice concussions (estimated 20% of total by prior research). Concussion remains a clinical diagnosis with known player non-disclosure bias. No direct injury mechanism data (game vs. practice) or medical clearance details were available. Severity could not be directly assessed; games missed used as proxy. The 38-percent reduction correlates with but cannot be causally attributed to the Targeting rule alone, as concurrent helmet technology changes and other rule modifications also occurred. Only one season of 17-game data exists, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: The 38% reduction in NFL concussions following the Targeting rule and kickoff rule changes validates that administrative and equipment controls meaningfully reduce head injury burden. NFL and college football programs should continue investing in helmet technology, enforce targeting penalties rigorously, and monitor concussion clusters in the final third of the season when fatigue-related risk increases. Defensive linemen require special attention: despite the lowest concussion rate, they miss the most games per event, suggesting more severe injuries when they occur — likely due to cumulative microtrauma. Staff should implement fatigue-monitoring protocols as the season progresses.
athleteDev: Positional concussion risk data provide critical guidance for athlete development programs. Tight ends and defensive backs face the highest rates of game-missing concussions, reinforcing the need for position-specific contact technique education and progressive load management during full-contact practices. Young defensive and offensive linemen should receive particular attention regarding head contact biomechanics and proper blocking technique to reduce the cumulative microtrauma associated with the high average games-missed observed in that group. BMI-related concussion clustering (peaks at BMI 27 and 38) suggests that body composition extremes warrant targeted monitoring.
rtp: Defensive linemen average nearly 4 games missed per concussion — the highest of any position — despite having the lowest concussion rate, underscoring that their concussions tend to be more severe or complex. Return-to-play timelines for this group should not be abbreviated based on position-group norms. The significant reduction in average games missed per concussion (from 3.46 in 2017-18 to ~2.14 in subsequent years) league-wide suggests that improved protocols and rule changes may be facilitating faster, safer returns. Medical staffs should be alert to elevated late-season concussion risk and factor cumulative season fatigue into RTP decision-making.

citation: Grashow RG, Roberts AL, Zafonte R, Pascual-Leone A, Taylor H, Baggish A, Nadler L, Courtney TK, Connor A, Weisskopf MG. Defining Exposures in Professional Football: Professional American-Style Football Players as an Occupational Cohort. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2019;7(2):2325967119829212. DOI 10.1177/2325967119829212
abstract: This narrative review proposes that professional American football players should be studied as an occupational cohort using the established frameworks of occupational epidemiology. The authors conducted a review of existing football research, occupational epidemiology methods, and occupational medicine studies to describe how the four-step occupational epidemiology framework (surveillance, exposure assessment, hypothesis testing, prevention/evaluation) could be applied to NFL players. The review identifies key biases inherent to football health research — including the healthy worker effect and survivor bias — and maps potential exposures across the pre-professional, professional, and post-retirement life stages of a football player.
tldr: This review argues that studying NFL players through an occupational epidemiology lens — treating football like a job with measurable workplace hazards — could unlock deeper understanding of how specific football activities cause long-term health outcomes like CTE, arthritis, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive impairment. Most existing football health research uses overly broad 'ever played football' exposure measures, which cannot isolate what actually causes harm. The review proposes position-specific exposure matrices, better comparison groups (other elite athletes rather than the general population), and strategies to address the healthy worker effect and recall bias. For performance staff, the practical implication is that position, total snaps, full-contact practice load, and training regimen are all quantifiable exposures that should be documented and monitored.
methods: Narrative review of existing football health research, occupational epidemiology methods papers, and occupational medicine studies. No primary data collected. Authors mapped a generalized occupational epidemiology workflow (Figure 1) and a hierarchy of hazard controls (Figure 2) onto professional football. Exposures were organized across life stages (pre-professional, professional, post-retirement) and categorized as game-related (total seasons, snaps, plays, position) or acute effects/mediators (concussions, joint injuries, medication use, sleep deprivation). Biases analyzed include healthy worker effect, volunteer/nonresponse bias, survivor bias, small-study bias, recall bias, and comparison group selection issues.
findings: Professional football players demonstrate a constellation of adverse health outcomes — cognitive dysfunction, neuroanatomical changes, joint injuries, cardiovascular disease, and neurodegenerative disease (CTE) — that are linked to football exposure but not yet fully causally mapped to specific activities. The healthy worker effect (HWE) inflates apparent longevity of NFL players compared to the general population, masking harmful effects; replacement-player comparison data suggest career players are ~38% more likely to have died during study follow-up than replacement players. Position is the most relevant subgroup variable and should be used in a position-exposure matrix (PEM). Current NFL safety interventions fall at the lower end of the hierarchy of controls (PPE and administrative controls), with no elimination or substitution strategies implemented. Only 62% of former players consistently recalled the same number of concussions at baseline and follow-up.
limitations: As a narrative review, no systematic search strategy or quantitative synthesis was performed. Many of the football health studies reviewed had small sample sizes subject to small-study bias. Historical exposure data for former players are largely unavailable or proprietary, making retrospective exposure reconstruction unreliable. The review does not provide new empirical data but rather a methodological framework. The influence of recall bias on published football health statistics is rarely explored systematically.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football organizations should begin systematically documenting and quantifying player exposures — snap counts, full-contact practice sessions, position changes, training load, medication use — using an occupational epidemiology framework. This data infrastructure would enable causal analysis linking specific football activities to health outcomes rather than relying on blunt 'ever played' measures. Safety interventions should progress up the hierarchy of controls beyond helmets and rule changes toward engineering and administrative controls (e.g., robotic tackling dummies, contact restrictions in practice). Position-specific health monitoring programs are justified by the evidence that injury and disease risk varies substantially by position.
athleteDev: The occupational framework highlights that football exposure begins pre-professionally (age of first exposure, high school/collegiate play) and that early cumulative exposure may independently predict long-term neurocognitive and musculoskeletal outcomes. Developmental programs should document position history, snap counts, and full-contact practice exposure from early in a player's career. The review supports limiting high-intensity contact in practice, particularly for developmental-stage athletes, as this represents an administrative control that reduces exposure without eliminating participation. Monitoring weight gain — a documented risk factor for cardiovascular outcomes — throughout a player's developmental arc is also warranted.
rtp: The occupational epidemiology framework is directly applicable to return-to-play contexts: just as occupational medicine uses job-exposure matrices to determine safe return to work after injury, a football position-exposure matrix (PEM) could guide RTP decisions by quantifying the specific physical demands of each position. The review's discussion of acute injury mediators — including medication use, sleep deprivation, and cumulative sub-concussive blows — as contributors to long-term outcomes underscores that RTP protocols should address the full exposure environment, not just the presenting injury. Recall bias in concussion self-report (only 62% consistency) has direct implications for clinical history-taking during RTP evaluation.

citation: DeFroda SF, Bodendorfer BM, Hartnett DA, Milner JD, Yang DS, Silber ZS, Forsythe B. Defining the Contemporary Epidemiology and Return to Play for High Ankle Sprains in the National Football League. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2021. DOI 10.1080/00913847.2021.1924046
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study examined 533 high ankle sprains (syndesmotic injuries) in 498 NFL players across 11 seasons (2009-10 to 2019-20) using publicly available data. The annual incidence increased linearly from 1.75 to 2.49 per 10,000 player exposures over the study period. Offensive players accounted for 57% of injuries, primarily offensive linemen (36.5%) and wide receivers (24%). Overall, 89.7% of players returned to play with an average return time of 80.5 ± 132.9 days; defensive players returned faster (68.1 days) than offensive players (90.1 days), and higher age at injury significantly increased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=1.24, p=0.0088).
tldr: High ankle sprains are rising in frequency in the NFL, now occurring at nearly 2.5 per 10,000 player exposures — up from 1.75 a decade ago — and represent a significant source of missed playing time at approximately 11 weeks per injury. Nine in 10 players successfully return to play, but offensive linemen and wide receivers carry the greatest positional burden. Older players at the time of injury are meaningfully more likely to fail RTP (OR=1.24 per year of age), while more experienced players are more likely to return. Defensive players return faster than offensive players. Performance staff should plan for ~11-week absences when a high ankle sprain occurs and recognize that younger/more experienced players have a clear RTP advantage.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury data from the 2009-10 through 2019-20 regular seasons using Pro-Football-Reference.com and ESPN. Injuries identified as 'high ankle sprain,' 'syndesmosis,' or 'syndesmotic injury.' Player characteristics recorded: age (mean 25.8 ± 3.1 yr), BMI (mean 31.8 ± 4.6), years of experience, and position. Injury setting (preseason/regular season/postseason) and timing documented. RTP defined as first date activated and played a preseason/training camp or regular season game. Incidence calculated per 10,000 athlete exposures (53 players/team, 105 practices/season, 20 games/season, 32 teams). Linear regression assessed incidence trends; multivariate logistic regression identified RTP failure risk factors (controlling for year of injury, position, BMI, age, experience). Kaplan-Meier survivorship plots computed for RTP.
findings: 533 high ankle sprains identified in 498 players. Incidence increased linearly from 1.75 to 2.49 per 10,000 player exposures (R²=0.3357). Offensive players: 304/533 injuries (57.0%); offensive linemen most affected (111/304, 36.5%), followed by wide receivers (73/304, 24%). Defensive secondary accounted for 86/228 (37.7%) of defensive injuries. 89.7% (478/533) of players returned to play; average RTP 80.5 ± 132.9 days. Defensive players returned faster (68.1 ± 114.6 days) than offensive players (90.1 ± 144.8 days, p=0.084). Higher age at injury increased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=1.24, 95% CI 1.05-1.46, p=0.0088). More years of experience decreased failure-to-RTP risk (OR=0.71, 95% CI 0.59-0.86, p=0.0004). Injuries occurred predominantly in the regular season (422/533, 79.2%).
limitations: Data sourced from public databases, which may result in over- or under-reporting; high-profile players may be overrepresented. Minor high ankle injuries with rapid recovery may be underreported, potentially inflating the average RTP time. Injury severity, imaging findings, and treatment type (operative vs. nonoperative) were not available, preventing severity-stratified analysis. Player injury history was unavailable, precluding analysis of re-injury risk. Variables such as playing surface and contact mechanism could not be assessed. Team rehabilitation protocols and personnel decisions influencing RTP could not be controlled.
practicalImplications: With an average RTP of ~11 weeks and a linearly increasing incidence rate, high ankle sprains represent one of the most significant sources of offensive player unavailability in the NFL. Programs should have standardized high ankle sprain management protocols that account for the full expected timeline rather than the 2-3 week timelines reported in older NCAA literature. Age at injury is an independent predictor of RTP failure — players 28+ years old warrant closer monitoring and more conservative RTP criteria. Offensive linemen and wide receivers are the highest-risk positional groups and should receive targeted preventive attention including ankle stability training and appropriate taping/bracing protocols.
athleteDev: High ankle sprains have a documented rising incidence in the NFL, likely related to increasing athlete size, speed, and strength — all of which are also developmental targets. This creates a tension where optimizing physical development may increase syndesmotic injury risk, particularly for offensive linemen who bear the highest absolute injury burden. Developmental programs should incorporate ankle dorsiflexion mobility, syndesmotic stability exercises, and progressive contact load management to prepare linemen and wide receivers for the high-force environments that cause these injuries. The injury peak in the regular season (79.2%) rather than preseason suggests that load and collision intensity — not conditioning — are primary drivers.
rtp: High ankle sprains require an average of 80.5 days for RTP in the NFL, approximately 3-4x longer than historically reported in NCAA studies, likely reflecting more severe injury profiles or higher return-to-performance standards at the professional level. The 90% RTP rate is encouraging, but the 10% failure rate — concentrated in older, less-experienced players — should inform realistic counseling at time of injury. Defensive players return approximately 22 days faster than offensive players (68.1 vs. 90.1 days), which may reflect positional demand differences or different operative management rates. Age at injury is the strongest modifiable risk predictor: each additional year of age increases failure-to-RTP odds by 24%. Conservative criteria and extended timelines should be applied for veteran players sustaining these injuries.

citation: Hill KP, Wu A, Solomon G, Mack C, Ling G, Mayer T, Sills A. Design Considerations in the National Football League and National Football League Players Association Prescription Drug Monitoring Program: An Introduction and Overview. Current Sports Medicine Reports. 2021;20(11):614-616.
abstract: This special communication describes the design and implementation of the NFL/NFLPA Prescription Drug Monitoring Program (PDMP), established in May 2019 as part of the collectively bargained Pain Management Committee (PMC). The PDMP monitors all prescriptions entered in the NFL Electronic Medical Record (EMR) for all 32 clubs, tracking opioids, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs, nonnarcotic analgesics, and other pain medications from training camp through the week following each club's last game. Biannual reports are issued to NFL and NFLPA medical leadership, with deidentified trend data shared with team medical staffs to inform evidence-based prescribing practices.
tldr: The NFL and NFLPA created the first league-wide prescription drug monitoring program in professional sports in 2019, mandating that all 32 teams document every pain medication prescription in a shared EMR system. The PDMP's goals are to understand prescribing patterns, identify potential opioid misuse, and guide evidence-based pain management — recognizing that NFL players face continuous cycles of performance, pain, and recovery that create unique opioid risk. For team medical and performance staff, this program means prescription practices are now tracked and benchmarked league-wide. Staff should be familiar with the PDMP's scope, use it to guide prescribing toward evidence-based alternatives to opioids, and treat it as a tool for player health rather than surveillance.
methods: Special communication describing the design and implementation process of the NFL/NFLPA PDMP. Origin: May 2019 NFL/NFLPA collective bargaining agreement mandated documentation of all player medications. IQVIA (independent health IT company) contracted to design data collection protocols and monitor real-time reporting. Four PDMP components: (1) prescription data collection via NFL EMR, (2) required club reporting from training camp through season end, (3) required player reporting of all prescription medications to club physicians, (4) biannual deidentified reports to league medical leadership and individual clubs. Drug classification uses Generic Product Identifier (GPI) groups mapped from FDA National Drug Codes: opioids, muscle relaxants, NSAIDs, nonnarcotic analgesics, corticosteroids, narcotic antitussives, nerve pain medications, antimigraine medications.
findings: The PDMP was the first of its kind in professional or amateur sports. It captures all prescriptions written for NFL players by club and unaffiliated physicians entered into the NFL EMR. Clubs can access their own aggregated prescribing patterns benchmarked against the rest of the league via an interactive dashboard. The PMC used PDMP data to develop educational resources on evidence-based alternatives to opioids, including cannabinoids, for both players and medical staffs. The program identified that completeness and accuracy depend on diligent manual entry by club medical staff, creating potential for incomplete capture. No outcome data on prescribing changes or opioid misuse rates are reported in this introductory overview.
limitations: The EMR is not the only route through which prescriptions can be ordered; some prescriptions must be entered manually by busy club medical staff who may not always be the primary prescriber. The EMR records prescriptions written but does not record whether the prescription was filled or taken. Injury diagnosis linking prescriptions to specific conditions may be missing in some records. No consensus definition exists for aberrant prescribing behavior, reducing the PDMP's ability to detect problematic prescribing patterns. The program was in early stages at publication, with data collection processes still evolving. Prescriptions from unaffiliated physicians must be self-reported by players to club staff for entry, creating a dependency on player compliance.
practicalImplications: NFL team medical and performance staff should understand that prescription practices for all pain medications — including NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, and opioids — are now monitored, benchmarked league-wide, and reported biannually to team and league medical leadership. This creates accountability and an opportunity to shift toward evidence-based alternatives to opioids for pain management. The PMC's educational resources on cannabinoids and non-opioid analgesics should be integrated into team pain management protocols. Staff should actively support complete and accurate EMR entry of all prescriptions to ensure the PDMP fulfills its patient safety mission. The program sets a precedent that could be adopted by college programs seeking to monitor prescription patterns in student-athletes.
athleteDev: While the PDMP is focused on professional players, its underlying principle — systematic monitoring of prescription pain medication use in the context of performance, pain, and recovery cycles — is equally applicable to college football programs. Young players entering high-load training environments are at risk for inappropriate opioid use patterns that can persist post-career. Development programs should implement education on opioid risks, establish clear non-opioid-first pain management protocols, and consider tracking prescription patterns as a component of player health documentation. The PMC's emphasis on educating players about evidence-based alternatives to opioids is a model that college programs should adopt proactively.
rtp: The PDMP's recognition that NFL players are engaged in 'continuous cycles of performance, pain, and recovery' is directly relevant to RTP contexts. Pain medication prescribing during injury rehabilitation is a critical period where opioid overuse patterns can develop. Team medical staff making RTP decisions should audit prescription patterns during rehabilitation — enabled by PDMP data — to ensure that pain management is not masking incomplete recovery or driving premature return. The shift toward evidence-based non-opioid alternatives (NSAIDs, nerve pain medications, cannabinoids) for managing post-injury pain during rehabilitation aligns with both the PDMP's goals and best-practice RTP medicine.
